response:
Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and User Datagram Protocol (UDP) both ride on top of IP, but they serve very different purposes. TCP focuses on accuracy and reliability, while UDP focuses on speed and simplicity. The key distinctions are summarized below.

TCP vs. UDP – Side-by-Side Comparison

| Feature / Aspect                | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)                              | UDP (User Datagram Protocol)                                   |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| Connection Model                | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake establishes a session)      | Connectionless (no handshake; “fire-and-forget”)               |
| Reliability                     | Guarantees delivery via acknowledgments (ACKs) and retransmits   | No delivery guarantees; lost packets are not retransmitted     |
| Ordering of Packets             | Ensures in-order delivery (sequence numbers)                     | No ordering; application must reorder if required              |
| Error Detection                 | Checksum + ACK/NAK + retransmission                              | Checksum only; corrupt packets are discarded                   |
| Flow Control                    | Yes (window size, sliding window)                                | No                                                             |
| Congestion Control              | Yes (slow start, congestion avoidance, etc.)                     | No                                                             |
| Header Size (without options)   | 20 bytes                                                         | 8 bytes                                                        |
| Throughput / Latency            | Higher overhead → typically lower raw speed, higher latency      | Minimal overhead → typically faster, lower latency             |
| Multicast / Broadcast           | Not natively supported                                           | Supported (IPv4 broadcast, IPv4/IPv6 multicast)                |
| Stream vs. Message              | Stream-oriented (byte stream)                                   | Message-oriented (datagrams preserved intact)                  |
| Packet Boundaries               | Not preserved; application must frame data                       | Preserved; one datagram = one message                          |
| Suitable Payload Size           | Up to ~65 535 bytes minus headers; usually segmented by TCP      | Up to 65 507 bytes minus headers; apps often keep <1 500 bytes |
| Typical Use Cases               | Web (HTTP/HTTPS), FTP, SSH, SMTP, Telnet, database access        | DNS queries, VoIP, video/ audio streaming (RTP), DHCP, gaming  |
| Overhead to Implement (Code)    | Higher (state machine, buffers, timers)                          | Lower (send/receive sockets)                                   |
| Security Options                | Commonly used with TLS/SSL                                       | Can also use DTLS, but less common                             |
| Connection Teardown             | Formal 4-way FIN handshake                                       | None; simply stop sending                                      |
| Suited For                      | Accuracy-critical, order-critical transfers                      | Real-time, loss-tolerant, or broadcast applications            |

When to choose which?

Use TCP when:
• You cannot tolerate missing or out-of-order data (file transfers, web pages, database traffic).  
• The application prefers the transport layer to handle retransmissions and congestion control automatically.  
• You want built-in mechanisms for flow control and connection management.

Use UDP when:
• Low latency is more important than perfect reliability (VoIP, live gaming, live video).  
• The application already includes its own loss-recovery or can cope with some packet loss.  
• You need multicast/broadcast distribution or a massive number of concurrent clients.

In short, TCP trades extra overhead for reliability and ordered delivery, whereas UDP trades reliability for speed and simplicity.
